name: Asuras (demi-gods/titans)
shortDescription: Asuras are powerful, often wrathful, demi-gods in Buddhist cosmology, known for their constant struggle for power and their envy of the Devas.
description: Asuras occupy a distinct realm in Buddhist cosmology, often translated as demi-gods, titans, or sometimes demons, although the latter is a misnomer as they are not inherently evil. Their defining characteristic is their insatiable desire for power and their constant conflict with the Devas (gods) for control of the celestial realms, particularly Mount Sumeru. The origin of the Asuras is complex and varies across different Buddhist texts. In some accounts, they are said to have been Devas who fell from grace due to their pride and craving for dominance. Other accounts depict them as beings born from the same cosmic egg as the Devas, but with a different temperament. Their realm is often portrayed as a place of constant strife and competition, reflecting their inner turmoil. While not entirely malevolent, the Asuras are driven by jealousy and a relentless pursuit of superiority, which often leads to suffering for themselves and others. Their existence serves as a reminder of the dangers of unchecked ambition and the importance of cultivating humility and contentment. They are a significant part of the Wheel of Life (Bhavachakra) representing a state of suffering and dissatisfaction. The battles between Asuras and Devas are a recurring theme in Buddhist mythology, symbolizing the internal struggles within individuals between positive and negative emotions, and the external conflicts that arise from ego and attachment.

sources.title: The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism
sources.author: Robert E. Buswell Jr. and Donald S. Lopez Jr.
